title: WebJ
text:
This is a website on using Julia with Quarto. It site is hosted on github for now - so ykwongsuss.github.io. The site is similar to WebR with basic examples on dataframes, plots and some programming.

types
dataframes
tables
plots
programming


1 Websites

ykwongWebR: R Basics
ykwongWebStats: Mathematical Statistics with R
ykwongWebSurv: Survival Analysis with R
ykwongWebNPar: NonParametric Statistics with R
ykwongWebLA: Linear Algebra with Julia



2  Notes

yaml: html, pdf
qmd: footnote, references
qblocks: code blocks, tex blocks and div blocks
qtex: selected tex codes
qD2: D2 diagramming

title: textEntry
text:
0.1 A table of space separated numbers

using DataFrames

title: types
text:
1 Simple Data Types in Julia
The usual basic types of integer, floats, boolean, character and strings are available.

typeof(3) |> println
typeof(3.14) |> println
typeof(pi) |> println
Bool(3.14==pi) |> println

Int64
Float64
Irrational{:π}
false



1.1 Tuples, Vectors and Arrays

# tuple
a = (1,2,'3') |> typeof |> println
# vector
b = [1,2,3] |> typeof |> println
# matrix
C = [1 2 3; 4 5 6] |> typeof |> println

Tuple{Int64, Int64, Char}
Vector{Int64}
Matrix{Int64}




1.2 Named Tuple


1.3 Strings

s = "Welcome Home"
lowercase(s) |> println
s[1] |> println

welcome home
W




1.4 Categorical and Numerical Data

1.4.1 Tables

# Data
students = ["Alice", "Bob", "Callie","Danny"]
marks = [84, 53, 66,  80]
grades = ['A', 'C', 'B', 'A'] 

4-element Vector{Char}:
 'A': ASCII/Unicode U+0041 (category Lu: Letter, uppercase)
 'C': ASCII/Unicode U+0043 (category Lu: Letter, uppercase)
 'B': ASCII/Unicode U+0042 (category Lu: Letter, uppercase)
 'A': ASCII/Unicode U+0041 (category Lu: Letter, uppercase)




1.4.2 TypedTable.jl

import Pkg; Pkg.add("TypedTables")
using TypedTables
  tTable = Table(
    names = students,
    marks = marks,
    grades = grades
  )
  tTable |> display
  tTable[1]

Table with 3 columns and 4 rows:
     names   marks  grades
   ┌──────────────────────
 1 │ Alice   84     A
 2 │ Bob     53     C
 3 │ Callie  66     B
 4 │ Danny   80     A


(names = "Alice", marks = 84, grades = 'A')




1.4.3 PrettyTables.jl

using PrettyTables



1.4.4 DataFrames.jl

using DataFrames
df = DataFrame(
   Names = students,
   Marks = marks,
   Grades = grades
)
df

4×3 DataFrame



Row
Names
Marks
Grades



String

Char

A

title: types
text:
1 Simple Data Types in Julia
The usual basic types of integer, floats, boolean, character and strings are available.

typeof(3) |> println
typeof(3.14) |> println
typeof(pi) |> println
typeof(3.14==pi) |> println
typeof("a string") |> println

Int64
Float64
Irrational{:π}
Bool
String



1.1 Tuples, Vectors and Arrays

# tuple
a = (1,2,'3') |> typeof |> println
# vector
b = [1,2,3] |> typeof |> println
# matrix
C = [1 2 3; 4 5 6] |> typeof |> println

Tuple{Int64, Int64, Char}
Vector{Int64}
Matrix{Int64}




1.2 Named Tuples

# as the name implies
nt = (a=1, b=2.0)
nt |> println
nt.a

(a = 1, b = 2.0)


1



# usage
for val in nt
  println(val)
end

1
2.0




1.3 Strings

s = "Welcome Home"
lowercase(s) |> println
s[1] |> println

welcome home
W




1.4 Dict

title: types
text:
0.1 Packages

import Pkg; Pkg.add("PrettyTables");
Pkg.add("DataFrames")



0.2 Categorical and Numerical Data

0.2.1 Tables

# Data
students = ["Alice", "Bob", "Callie","Danny"]
marks = [84, 53, 66,  80]
grades = ['A', 'C', 'B', 'A'] 

4-element Vector{Char}:
 'A': ASCII/Unicode U+0041 (category Lu: Letter, uppercase)
 'C': ASCII/Unicode U+0043 (category Lu: Letter, uppercase)
 'B': ASCII/Unicode U+0042 (category Lu: Letter, uppercase)
 'A': ASCII/Unicode U+0041 (category Lu: Letter, uppercase)




0.2.2 TypedTable.jl

import Pkg; Pkg.add("TypedTables")
using TypedTables
  tTable = Table(
    names = students,
    marks = marks,
    grades = grades
  )
  tTable |> display
  tTable[1]

Table with 3 columns and 4 rows:
     names   marks  grades
   ┌──────────────────────
 1 │ Alice   84     A
 2 │ Bob     53     C
 3 │ Callie  66     B
 4 │ Danny   80     A


(names = "Alice", marks = 84, grades = 'A')


(Note: pretty_tables (pkg: PrettyTable) does not format well in quarto. ).


0.2.3 DataFrames.jl

using DataFrames
df = DataFrame(
   Names = students,
   Marks = marks,
   Grades = grades
)
df |> Table

Table with 3 columns and 4 rows:
     Names   Marks  Grades
   ┌──────────────────────
 1 │ Alice   84     A
 2 │ Bob     53     C
 3 │ Callie  66     B
 4 │ Danny   80     A

title: textEntry in Julia
text:
For general data reading in Julia, DataFrames.jl is widely used. Additionally, CSV.jl is often used in conjunction with DataFrames.jl for reading CSV files.

0.1 Data
name   sex
Alice   F
Bob     M
Callie  F
Danny   M


0.2 1. Vectors to DataFrame

using DataFrames

df = DataFrame("name"=>["Alice","Bob","Callie","Danny"],
               "sex"=>["F","M","F","M"])
df

4×2 DataFrame



Row
name
sex



String
String

F

F


4
Danny
M







mat = ["Alice" 84; "Bob" 53; "Callie" 66; "Danny" 80]
score = ["name", "marks"]
df2 = DataFrame(mat, score)
df2

title: import Export to DataFrames
text:
For general data reading in Julia, DataFrames.jl is widely used. Additionally, CSV.jl is often used in conjunction with DataFrames.jl for reading CSV files.

0.1 1. Entered numbers

using DataFrames

a = [1,2,3,4]
b = ["M","F","F","M"]
DataFrame(a=1:4, b=["M", "F", "F", "M"])

4×2 DataFrame



Row
a
b



Int64
String




1
1
M


2
2
F


3
3
F


4
4
M








0.2 2. Cut and Pasted

title: quartoJ
text:
Julia is supported by Quarto through Julia code chunk (type ```julia or use /julia ).
{julia}
1+1

1+1

2



0.1 Julia version
# in terminal type:
julia --version


0.2 Add Package
{julia}
#| warning: FALSE
import Pkg; Pkg.add("Distributions")

import Pkg; Pkg.add("Distributions")



0.3 Using Julia Package
Figure 1 below as an example.

using Plots
plot(sin, 
     x->sin(2x), 
     0, 2π, 
     leg=false, 
     fill=(0,:lavender))
plot!(size=(300,200))






  
    
  



  
    
  



  
    
  




























Figure 1: Parametric Plots






0.4 Using Pipe

using Pipe
# allows for _underscore as placeholder for var
@pipe pi |> round(_, digits=4) |> println

3.1416
